course_title: Psychosocial Aspects of Occupational Therapy Practice
course_description:
This course builds on the psychiatric and mental health foundations provided in OCTY2105. Content addresses information gathering and service delivery processes in psychosocial fields of adult occupational therapy, including: mental health, physical health, ageing and intellectual disability. A specific focus is on understanding the impact of psychological, social, emotional, and behavioural factors on the individual experience of a diversity of physical and mental health conditions.

The purpose of this course is to develop student knowledge and skills in working with people for whom psychosocial factors (mental illness, intellectual disability, dementia, trauma etc) affect occupational performance. Students will obtain experience in the occupational therapy process including: the therapeutic relationship, assessment, clinical reasoning, and different approaches to service provision. Students will be able to select, administer and interpret appropriate assessments relevant to people with psychosocial concerns, and formulate an intervention plan of client needs using a person-centred, strengths-based, recovery-focussed approach. Students will choose and apply an occupational therapy model of practice to develop assessment and design intervention strategies, and will consider ways in which psychosocial problems can influence a person’s occupational performance, and engaging in occupations can address psychosocial concerns.

incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Timetable details are posted on LearnUQ. Please note that there are several components of this course on the timetable, including lectures, tutorials, sessional placements (including briefing and debriefing sessions), and placement orientation. Students are expected to attend all scheduled placement sessions, including Week 1 orientation. Details for these sessions are on the Timetable.
Please note that lectures in this course are delivered pre-recorded, however, to support student engagement in learning activities, a scheduled lecture time is included in the Timetable. However, students are expected to independently manage workload so they can watch weekly lecture recordings before attending tutorial sessions. The recordings are made available to students on LearnUQ.
course_aims: This course focuses on understanding the strengths and challenges of individuals who have disability, in particular intellectual disability, and/or mental illness. This course encourages students to pay particular attention to the ways in which occupational therapists might support these individuals to achieve their personal, social and vocational goals that are related to psychosocial health and wellbeing.

learning_outcomes.description: Act in a professional manner that aligns with the standards and values of occupational therapy profession, including maintenance of professional requirements.
Identify and apply the frames of reference and theories relevant to psychosocial practice in occupational therapy.
Identify and analyse the interdependence between physical, psychosocial and mental health fields of practice.

Identify the importance of the roles played by significant others (e.g. family members) and explain the potential impact of the client's behavioural, social and emotional challenges on significant others.
Analyse and evaluate the factors that contribute to the development and maintenance of a range of behavioural, social and emotional challenges in adulthood.
Identify and use a range of formal and informal information gathering approaches used in the fields of psychosocial practice.
Apply and evaluate appropriate approaches to improving occupational performance and participation of individuals and families where there are social-emotional and/or behavioural challenges.

Evaluate the best available evidence to generate clinical decisions when working with people with social, emotional, and/or behavioural challenges, those with mental health issues, and those experiencing challenging transitions.

Use and carry-out a range of professional and effective communication skills in developing and maintaining therapeutic and professional relationships, including ability to effectively work with others.
Reflect on and provide critical appraisal of own and others performance as required and implement appropriate changes in response to this appraisal.

assessment_details.title: Occupational Formulation Report
assessment_details.task_description: Students are required to write an occupational formulation report, focusing on the person's psychosocial health and well-being. This is an individual assessment and not a must-pass assessment. Further information is available on Learn.UQ. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Turnitin on LearnUQ.
Coversheet
Students are required to use the assessment coversheet provided on the course Blackboard site (Learn.UQ). This coversheet is designed to guide your understanding of appropriate and inappropriate use of AI and MT tools in academic work.
The completed coversheet must be attached to your assignment upon submission. You are required to document and declare any use of AI or MT tools in the preparation of your assessment.
Formatting Requirements
As outlined in the task description, a word limit has been specified for this assignment. This limit is intended to support students in developing their report writing skills in a professional and succinct manner, which is a transferable skill in any clinical context.
Please note that any content submitted beyond the specified page limit will not be marked.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Please refer to the relevant UQ policy.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Please refer to the relevant university policy.

Students will be required to complete a quiz to demonstrate their foundational knowledge of Mental State Examination and their ability to apply the knowledge to a case. Further information will be provided on Learn UQ. In the event of a disruption that prevents the scheduled assessment from occurring as planned, the assessment will be changed to an alternative form of assessment, such as a Blackboard exam. The timing of the assessment may also be impacted. Students are expected to check connectivity prior to the online quiz to ensure they can complete this assessment. If they experience issues with connection, they will be required to submit evidence (such as screenshot). These affected students with appropriate evidence, then, may be given another opportunity to complete this quiz. This is not a must-pass assessment. assessment_details.title: Sessional placement performance
assessment_details.due_date: 28/07/2025 - 14/10/2025 Block 1 students: Submit the completed School Coversheet to Turnitin by Tuesday 26th August. Block 2 students: Submit the completed School Coversheet to Turnitin by Tuesday 14th Oct. If a completed coversheet is not submitted by the due date, your clinical educator will be unable to mark your performance.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Work integrated learning, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment pertains to the sessional placement. Please note the sessional placement includes the orientation session in Week 1, and 5-week performance (including briefing/debriefing sessions that occur on site). Professional behaviour: Students will be assessed on their performance across the duration of their sessional placement, including orientation. Aspects of performance that will be assessed include professional behaviour, self-management (including time management, organisation, reliability, and stress management), communication, information-gathering skills, and if applicable, service provision and documentation. Please note that this assessment is a must-pass, Pass/Fail assessment. This assessment has been approved to be exempt from offering a supplementary assessment. Use of AI in Student Placements Use of AI in Student Placements Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Large Language Models (e.g., ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Claude) is increasingly used in society. In terms of clinical placements, students are not permitted to use AI unless explicitly indicated by the provider that this use is permitted, and the parameters of use are clear. If these are not explicit, students are expected to consult with their practice educators before implementation to determine the acceptability of generative AI use and ensure that any potential risks associated with the intended use are addressed. AI is not a replacement of, or substitute for, professional reasoning or clinical skills. Human oversight is essential in its use, and to employ generative AI effectively and responsibly, students must understand how AI works, including its functions, data storage methods, and its impact on adherence to professional codes of 2 conduct. If used on placement, students are responsible for any outputs generated by AI, such as clinical notes and reports, and will be held accountable for the actions of any AI that is used in clinical practice. This responsibility includes ensuring adherence to the relevant profession's code of conduct and ethical standards. Transparency about generative AI use is essential in placement environments. Students must follow the relevant policies and procedures of their placement provider. Students are reminded that they need to demonstrate their ability to meet the inherent requirements of the relevant profession, operate within its scope of practice, and ensure that client/patient privacy and confidentiality are maintained at all times. Under no circumstances should identifiable clinical information be uploaded to open or external AI platforms, as it is unethical and irresponsible Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit via Turnitin. Please submits the completed School Coversheet by the due date. This ensures your CE can complete the marking on Blackboard.
Submitting the completed assessment coversheet by the due date forms part of the professional behaviour expected in this assessment. This requirement is reflected in the assessment rubric under the criteria for Professional Behaviour and Self-Management Skills.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
This assessment has been approved to be exempt from offering a supplementary assessment.

In terms of clinical placements, students are not permitted to use AI unless explicitly indicated by the provider that this use is permitted, and the parameters of use are clear. If these are not explicit, students are expected to consult with their practice educators before implementation to determine the acceptability of generative AI use and ensure that any potential risks associated with the intended use are addressed. Students are reminded that they need to demonstrate their ability to meet the inherent requirements of the relevant profession, operate within its scope of practice, and ensure that client/patient privacy and confidentiality are maintained at all times. Under no circumstances should identifiable clinical information be uploaded to open or external AI platforms, as it is unethical and irresponsible

assessment_details.title: End Semester Theory Exam (Inspera)
assessment_details.due_date: End of Semester Exam Period 8/11/2025 - 22/11/2025 The Central Exams will schedule the exam timetable. Please check the timetable once it is made available through the University system.
assessment_details.task_description: Details of the exam format will be provided on LearnUQ. The examination will cover the content of all lectures, tutorials and readings. The exam does not cover the content that is introduced in the placement related orientation and briefing/debriefing sessions. This exam will include case-based short answer responses to questions. Exam will be conducted via Inspera online (on campus) . In the event of disruption during the end of semester exam period that prevents the schedule assessment occurring as planned, the assessment will be changed to an alternative form of assessments, such as a Blackboard exam. The timing of the assessment may also be impacted. This is not a must-pass assessment. This assessment task is to be completed in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time 10 minutes Duration 120 minutes Calculator options No calculators permitted Open/closed book Closed book examination - no written materials permitted Exam platform Inspera Invigilation Invigilated in person
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Where an assessment item requires the student to "List 'x' number" of responses (for example, "List 4 performance areas" to gather further information), only the first 'x' responses listed by the student will be assessed. Any further responses listed will not be assessed.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
Late submission
Exams submitted after the end of the submission time will incur a late penalty .

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (28 Jul - 02 Nov)
learning_activities.topic: Pre-recorded Weekly Lecture There is one two-hour of pre-recorded lecture per week. The recordings will be made available on Learn.UQ. To support student engagement in learning activities, a scheduled lecture time is included in the Timetable. However, students are expected to independently manage workload so they can watch weekly lecture recordings before attending tutorial sessions. The detailed lecture schedule is available on Learn.UQ. Use of AI/MT and copyright Students are reminded that lecture notes and recordings provided in this course have been developed by the University of Queensland and/or third-party contributors. Uploading any part of these materials, including transcripts or audio content, to external platforms, artificial intelligence (AI) tools, or machine translation (MT) tools may constitute a breach of copyright. Please ensure that you use all course materials responsibly and in accordance with the University's academic integrity and copyright policies. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L06

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (28 Jul - 02 Nov)
learning_activities.topic: Weekly Tutorial These weekly 2-hour tutorials will extend the content of lectures, provide additional content, and increase practical engagement. During tutorials, students are expected to apply the knowledge introduced in relevant weekly lecture. Tutorials will be delivered as a face-to-face session and will not be recorded. Please note that active participation within the tutorials is essential for the successful completion of ALL assessment items. Due to the interactive nature, tutorials will not be recorded. However, due to the unforeseen circumstances (e.g. restrictions related to COVID), the delivery mode may be changed to online. The detailed tutorial schedule is available on Learn.UQ. Use of AI/MT and copyright Students are reminded that tutorial notes and other contents provided in this course have been developed by the University of Queensland and/or third-party contributors. Uploading any part of these materials to external platforms, artificial intelligence (AI) tools, or machine translation (MT) tools may constitute a breach of copyright. Please ensure that you use all course materials responsibly and in accordance with the University's academic integrity and copyright policies. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08, L09

Placement Orientation This is a one-off placement orientation. All students are expected to attend this orientation session. Please attend the orientation for your allocated placement (See InPlace for further information). Further information will become available on LearnUQ. This is a face-to-face session. However, due to unforeseen circumstances (e.g. restrictions related to COVID), the delivery mode may be changed to online. Both Block 1 and 2 students are to attend this session. If students are unable to attend, they are expected to contact their practice educator (see InPlace) prior to the orientation. Absentee Process If you are unable to attend for a legitimate reason (i.e., medical or extenuating circumstances), you are required to email a completed absentee form (available on Learn.UQ) to the School of Health and Rehabilitation Sciences Student and Academic Support Unit ( enquiries.shrs@uq.edu.au ). The absentee form to complete is located in Blackboard/Learning Resources/Course Information Folder. Please remember to cc your Course Coordinator and Clinical Educator into the email and attach your required evidence. The Student and Academic Support Unit will respond to you via email outlining whether you have been approved, and they will inform course staff of the outcome. Failure to communicate one's absence in advance without suitable medical or extenuating circumstances will result in not meeting the requirements of this course and will be reflected in the Professional performance assessment rubric under 'professional behaviour' and 'self-management'. Learning outcomes: L01, L09, L10

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 2 To Week 11 (04 Aug - 19 Oct)
learning_activities.topic: Sessional placement All students are required to participate in this sessional placement (i.e. up to 15 hours in total). This placement counts towards the 1000 placement hours required by the World Federation of Occupational Therapists to be accumulated during your degree. Due to the changing nature of service providers' situations, this planned learning activity may be modified, for example, on-line case-based learning experiences or simulation-based learning experiences on the UQ campus. Students will be allocated to either Block 1 or 2. Please note that unless students meet the placement compliance requirements, they will not be able to commence their placement . Due to the complexity of offering sessional placements, including enrolment number and service providers' capacity, make-up hours will not be available if students miss their placement activities. Prior to each session, students may need to complete online modules. Placement attendance Students are expected to attend all scheduled placement sessions, which may total up to 15 hours. Absences may be approved under specific circumstances, including: Medical grounds Exceptional circumstances Student Access Plan Absences will not be approved where the School determines that reasonable steps were not taken to avoid the circumstances. Examples of non-approved reasons include: Holiday arrangements Misreading the time or date Social or leisure events Work commitments Students should refer to the School’s Absentee Form for a full list of non-permissible circumstances and further guidance. Unapproved absences will be considered in the Professional Performance assessment of the placement, particularly under the criteria of Professional Behaviour and Self-Management Skills . Absentee Process If you are unable to attend for a legitimate reason (i.e., medical or extenuating circumstances), you are required to email a completed absentee form (available on Learn.UQ) to the School of Health and Rehabilitation Sciences Student and Academic Support Unit ( enquiries.shrs@uq.edu.au ). The absentee form to complete is located in Blackboard/Learning Resources/Course Information Folder. Please remember to cc your Clinical Educator into the email and attach your required evidence. The Student and Academic Support Unit will respond to you via email outlining whether you have been approved, and they will inform course staff of the outcome. Failure to communicate one's absence in advance without suitable medical or extenuating circumstances will result in not meeting the requirements of this course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08, L09, L10

Week 13 Meet the service user Seminar It offers a valuable opportunity to learn directly from individuals with lived experience of mental health challenges. Members of the Stepping Stone Clubhouse will be joining us to share their deeply personal perspectives and experiences. You will also have the opportunity to ask questions and engage in meaningful dialogue to deepen your understanding of mental health practice. Compulsory Seminar Attendance Attendance at this seminar is compulsory and it forms part of the sessional placement hours (i.e., WFOT 1000 hours). This activity occurs during the scheduled tutorial time (i.e., Wednesday). Please attend your usual tutorial session. Dress Code for Seminar Attendance Students are required to dress in smart casual attire for this seminar. Occupational Therapy uniforms should NOT be worn. Clothing such as exercise wear, or training gear, is not considered appropriate for this session. As this seminar is treated as part of your placement hours, students who arrive in unsuitable clothing may be asked to change before participating. Please ensure your attire reflects the professional standards expected in clinical and community settings. Absentee Process If you are unable to attend for a legitimate reason (i.e., medical or extenuating circumstances), you are required to email a completed absentee form (available on Learn.UQ) to the School of Health and Rehabilitation Sciences Student and Academic Support Unit ( enquiries.shrs@uq.edu.au ). The absentee form to complete is located in Blackboard/Learning Resources/Course Information Folder. Please remember to cc your Course Coordinator into the email and attach your required evidence. With approval, you will be asked to complete make-up activities by the due date. The Student and Academic Support Unit will respond to you via email outlining whether you have been approved, and they will inform course staff of the outcome. Failure to complete the make-up activities without suitable medical or extenuating circumstances will result in not meeting the requirements of this course. Learning outcomes: L01, L05, L09, L10

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . You'll also need to be aware of the following policies and procedures while completing this course: Working with Children Policy Fitness to Practise Policy Vaccinations and Immunisation Procedure Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy School guidelines Your school has additional guidelines you'll need to follow for this course: Resources for Current Students Learning activities
